description_plain: Your child can group flat shapes based on their properties in a way that shows which shapes belong to bigger or smaller groups.

subject: Math
description: Understand that attributes belonging to a category of two-dimensional figures also belong to all subcategories of that category.
description_plain: Your child understands that if a shape has certain features, then all shapes in smaller groups within that category also have those features.

subject: Math
description: Classify two-dimensional figures into categories based on their own properties.
description_plain: Your child can sort flat shapes into groups based on their features.

subject: Math
description: Represent real world mathematical problems by graphing points in the first quadrant of the coordinate plane, and interpret coordinate values of points in the context of the situation.
description_plain: Your child can plot points on a graph in the first quadrant and understand what the points mean in real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: Use a pair of perpendicular number lines, called axes, to define a coordinate system, with the intersection of the lines (the origin) arranged to coincide with the 0 on each line and a given point in the plane located by using an ordered pair of numbers, called its coordinates. Understand that the first number indicates how far to travel from the origin in the direction of one axis, and the second number indicates how far to travel in the direction of the second axis, with the convention that the names of the two axes and the coordinates correspond (e.g., x-axis and x-coordinate, y-axis and y-coordinate).
description_plain: Your child can use two number lines that cross at zero to find points on a grid by using pairs of numbers that tell how far to go along each line.

subject: Math
description: Graph points on the coordinate plane to solve real- world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use graphs to solve math and real-world problems.

subject: Math
description: Recognize volume as additive. Find volumes of solid figures composed of two non-overlapping right rectangular prisms by adding the volumes of the non- overlapping parts, applying this technique to solve real world problems.
description_plain: Your child knows that the volume of a solid made of two rectangular boxes is the sum of their volumes and can use this to solve real problems.

subject: Math
description: Apply the formulas V =(l)(w)(h) and V = (b)(h) for rectangular prisms to find volumes of right rectangular prisms with whole-number edge lengths in the context of solving real world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child uses formulas to find the volume of rectangular boxes with whole-number edges in real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the volume of a right rectangular prism with whole- number side lengths by packing it with unit cubes, and show that the volume is the same as would be found by multiplying the edge lengths, equivalently by multiplying the height by the area of the base. Represent three-fold whole-number products as volumes, e.g., to represent the associative property of multiplication.
description_plain: Your child finds the volume of rectangular boxes by counting cubes and by multiplying side lengths, showing these methods give the same answer.

subject: Math
description: Relate volume to the operations of multiplication and addition, and solve real world and mathematical problems involving volume.
description_plain: Your child connects volume to multiplication and addition and solves problems about volume.

subject: Math
description: Measure volumes by counting unit cubes, using cubic cm, cubic in, cubic ft, and improvised units.
description_plain: Your child can measure volume by counting unit cubes and using units like cubic centimeters, inches, feet, or other units.

subject: Math
description: A solid figure which can be packed without gaps or overlaps using n unit cubes is said to have a volume of n cubic units.
description_plain: Your child understands that a solid filled exactly with a certain number of unit cubes has a volume equal to that number of cubes.

subject: Math
description: A cube with side lengths 1 unit, called a "unit cube", is said to have "one cubic unit" of volume, and can be used to measure volume.
description_plain: Your child knows that a cube with sides of length 1 unit has a volume of one cubic unit and can use it to measure volume.

subject: Math
description: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement.
description_plain: Your child understands that volume is a property of solid shapes and learns how to measure it.

subject: Math
description: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of volume and relate volume to multiplication and to addition.
description_plain: Your child learns about volume and how it connects to multiplication and addition.

subject: Math
description: Make a line plot to display a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit (½, ¼, 1/8), Use operations for this grade to solve problems involving information presented in line plots.
description_plain: Your child can make a line plot to show measurements with fractions and use it to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Convert among different-sized standard measurement units within a given measurement system (e.g., convert 5 cm to 0.05 m).
description_plain: Your child can change measurements from one unit to another within the same system (like centimeters to meters) and use these changes to solve multi-step real-world problems.

subject: Math
description: Convert like measurement units within a given measurement system.
description_plain: Your child can convert between units that measure the same thing within one measurement system.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world problems involving division of unit fractions by non-zero whole numbers and division of whole numbers by unit fractions, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life problems by dividing fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by fractions, using pictures or equations to show their work.

subject: Math
description: Interpret division of a whole number by a unit fraction, and compute such quotients.
description_plain: Your child can understand and calculate what happens when a whole number is divided by a unit fraction.

subject: Math
description: Interpret division of a unit fraction by a non-zero whole number, and compute such quotients.
description_plain: Your child can understand and calculate what happens when a fraction with 1 in the numerator is divided by a whole number.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of division to divide unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions. Interpret division of a unit fraction by a non-zero whole number, and compute such quotients.
description_plain: Your child can divide unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions using strategies based on multiplication and division.

subject: Math
description: Solve real world problems involving multiplication of fractions and mixed numbers, e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child solves real-world problems by multiplying fractions and mixed numbers using pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Explaining why multiplying a given number by a fraction greater than one results in a product greater than the given number (recognizing multiplication by whole numbers greater than 1 as a familiar case); explaining why multiplying a given number by a fraction less than 1 results in a product smaller than the given number; and relating the principle of fraction equivalence a/b = (n × a)/ (n × b) to the effect of multiplying (a/b) by 1.
description_plain: Your child explains why multiplying by a fraction greater than one makes a number bigger, and multiplying by a fraction less than one makes it smaller.

subject: Math
description: Comparing the size of a product to the size of one factor on the basis of the size of the other factor, without performing the indicated multiplication.
description_plain: Your child can compare the size of a multiplication result to one of the numbers being multiplied by looking at the other number, without actually multiplying.

subject: Math
description: Interpret multiplication as scaling (resizing) by:
description_plain: Your child can understand multiplication as making a number bigger or smaller, like resizing something.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of a rectangle with fractional side lengths by tiling it with unit squares of the appropriate unit fraction side lengths, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths. Multiply fractional side lengths to find areas of rectangles, and represent fraction products as rectangular areas.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of rectangles with fractional side lengths by counting small squares and by multiplying the side lengths, showing that both methods give the same answer.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the product (a/b) × q as a part of a partition of q into b equal parts; equivalently, as the result of a sequence of operations a × q ÷ b.
description_plain: Your child understands multiplying a fraction by a number as taking part of that number or doing multiplication and division steps.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction or whole number by a fraction.
description_plain: Your child can multiply fractions and whole numbers by fractions, building on what they already know about multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Interpret a fraction as division of the numerator by the denominator (a/b = a ÷ b). Solve word problems involving division of whole numbers leading to answers in the form of fractions or mixed numbers, e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction means dividing the top number by the bottom number and can solve word problems that result in fractions or mixed numbers using pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to multiply and divide fractions.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide fractions by building on what they already know about multiplication and division.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole, including cases of unlike denominators, e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem. Use benchmark fractions and number sense of fractions to estimate mentally and assess the reasonableness of answers.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that add or subtract fractions with different bottom numbers by using pictures or equations and can estimate answers to check if they make sense.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract fractions with unlike denominators (including mixed numbers) by replacing given fractions with equivalent fractions in such a way as to produce an equivalent sum or difference of fractions with like denominators.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract fractions with different bottom numbers (including mixed numbers) by changing them to equivalent fractions with the same bottom number.

subject: Math
description: Use equivalent fractions as a strategy to add and subtract fractions.
description_plain: Your child can use equivalent fractions to help add and subtract fractions.

subject: Math
description: Add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals to hundredths, using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals to two decimal places using drawings or place value strategies, and can check if their answers make sense by estimating.

subject: Math
description: Find whole-number quotients of whole numbers with up to four-digit dividends and two-digit divisors, using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can divide large whole numbers (up to four digits) by two-digit numbers using place value and other strategies, and can explain their work with equations or pictures.

subject: Math
description: Fluently multiply multi-digit whole numbers using the standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can multiply multi-digit whole numbers quickly and accurately using the standard method.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round decimals to any place.
description_plain: Your child uses place value to round decimals to any place.

subject: Math
description: Perform operations with multi-digit whole numbers and with decimals to hundredths.
description_plain: Your child can work with large whole numbers and decimals to the hundredths place using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.

subject: Math
description: Compare two decimals to thousandths based on meanings of the digits in each place, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can compare two decimals to the thousandths place using >, =, and < symbols.

subject: Math
description: Read and write decimals to thousandths using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form, e.g. 347.392 = 3 × 100 + 4 × 10 + 7 × 1 + 3 × (1/10) + 9 × (1/100) + 2 × (1/1000)
description_plain: Your child can read and write decimals to the thousandths place using numbers, words, and expanded form to show the value of each digit.

subject: Math
description: Read, write, and compare decimals to thousandths.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and compare decimals up to the thousandths place.

subject: Math
description: Explain patterns in the number of zeros of the product when multiplying a number by powers of 10, and explain patterns in the placement of the decimal point when a decimal is multiplied or divided by a power of 10. Use whole number exponents to denote powers of 10.
description_plain: Your child can explain patterns when multiplying or dividing by powers of 10, like how zeros or decimal points move, and use exponents to show powers of 10.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that in a multi-digit number, a digit in one place represents 10 times as much as it represents in the place to its right and 1/10 of what it represents in the place to its left.
description_plain: Your child understands that each place in a number is ten times bigger than the place to its right and one-tenth the place to its left.

subject: Math
description: Understand the place value system.
description_plain: Your child understands how place value works in numbers.

subject: Math
description: Generate two numerical patterns using two given rules. Identify apparent relationships between corresponding terms. Form ordered pairs consisting of corresponding terms from the two patterns, and graph the ordered pairs on a coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can create two number patterns from two rules, find relationships between the numbers in the patterns, make pairs from matching numbers, and graph these pairs on a coordinate plane.

subject: Math
description: Analyze patterns and relationships.

description: Understand that a function from one set (called the domain) to another set (called the range) assigns to each element of the domain exactly one element of the range. If f is a function and x is an element of its domain, then f(x) denotes the output of f corresponding to the input x. The graph of f is the graph of the equation y = f(x).
description_plain: Your child understands that a function pairs each input with exactly one output, and knows how to read and graph these pairs using function notation.

description: Know there is a complex number i such that i² =-1, and every complex number has the form a + bi with a and b real.
description_plain: Your child knows that the imaginary unit i satisfies i² = -1 and that any complex number looks like a + bi, where a and b are real numbers.

subject: Math
description: Know there is a complex number i such that i² =-1, and every complex number has the form a + bi with a and b real.
description_plain: Your child knows that the imaginary unit i satisfies i² = -1 and that any complex number looks like a + bi, where a and b are real numbers.

subject: Math
description: Know there is a complex number i such that i² =-1, and every complex number has the form a + bi with a and b real.
description_plain: Your child knows that the imaginary unit i satisfies i² = -1 and that any complex number looks like a + bi, where a and b are real numbers.

subject: Math
description: Know there is a complex number i such that i² =-1, and every complex number has the form a + bi with a and b real.
description_plain: Your child knows that the imaginary unit i satisfies i² = -1 and that any complex number looks like a + bi, where a and b are real numbers.

subject: Math
description: Perform arithmetic operations with complex numbers.
description_plain: Your child can do addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with complex numbers.

description_plain: Your child can do addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with complex numbers.

description_plain: Your child can do addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with complex numbers.

subject: Math
description: Rewrite expressions involving radicals and rational exponents using the properties of exponents.
description_plain: Your child can rewrite expressions with roots and fractional exponents by using the rules for exponents.

subject: Math
description: Rewrite expressions involving radicals and rational exponents using the properties of exponents.
description_plain: Your child can rewrite expressions with roots and fractional exponents by using the rules for exponents.

subject: Math
description: Rewrite expressions involving radicals and rational exponents using the properties of exponents.
description_plain: Your child can rewrite expressions with roots and fractional exponents by using the rules for exponents.

subject: Math
description: Rewrite expressions involving radicals and rational exponents using the properties of exponents.
description_plain: Your child can rewrite expressions with roots and fractional exponents by using the rules for exponents.

subject: Math
description: Explain how the definition of the meaning of rational exponents follows from extending the properties of integer exponents to those values, allowing for a notation for radicals in terms of rational exponents.
description_plain: Your child understands that fractional exponents are a way to write roots, based on extending the rules for whole number exponents.

subject: Math
description: Explain how the definition of the meaning of rational exponents follows from extending the properties of integer exponents to those values, allowing for a notation for radicals in terms of rational exponents.
description_plain: Your child understands that fractional exponents are a way to write roots, based on extending the rules for whole number exponents.

subject: Math
description: Explain how the definition of the meaning of rational exponents follows from extending the properties of integer exponents to those values, allowing for a notation for radicals in terms of rational exponents.
description_plain: Your child understands that fractional exponents are a way to write roots, based on extending the rules for whole number exponents.

subject: Math
description: Explain how the definition of the meaning of rational exponents follows from extending the properties of integer exponents to those values, allowing for a notation for radicals in terms of rational exponents.
description_plain: Your child understands that fractional exponents are a way to write roots, based on extending the rules for whole number exponents.

subject: Math
description: Partition shapes into parts with equal areas. Express the area of each part as a unit fraction of the whole.
description_plain: Your child can divide shapes into equal parts and describe each part as a fraction of the whole shape.

subject: Math
description: Understand that shapes in different categories (e.g., rhombuses, rectangles, and others) may share attributes (e.g., having four sides), and that the shared attributes can define a larger category (e.g., quadrilaterals). Recognize rhombuses, rectangles, and squares as examples of quadrilaterals, and draw examples of quadrilaterals that do not belong to any of these subcategories.
description_plain: Your child can recognize that shapes like rhombuses, rectangles, and squares share features like having four sides, and can sort and compare shapes by their sides and angles, including drawing quadrilaterals that don't fit into those groups.

subject: Math
description: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
description_plain: Your child can think about shapes and their features.

subject: Math
description: Solve real world and mathematical problems involving perimeters of polygons, including finding the perimeter given the side lengths, finding an unknown side length, and exhibiting rectangles with the same perimeter and different areas or with the same area and different perimeters.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about the perimeter of shapes by adding side lengths, finding missing sides, and comparing rectangles with the same perimeter or area.

subject: Math
description: Geometric measurement: recognize perimeter as an attribute of plane figures and distinguish between linear and area measures.
description_plain: Your child understands that perimeter measures the distance around a shape and can tell the difference between length and area measurements.

subject: Math
description: Recognize area as additive. Find areas of rectilinear figures by decomposing them into non-overlapping rectangles and adding the areas of the non-overlapping parts, applying this technique to solve real world problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of shapes made of rectangles by breaking them apart and adding the areas, and use this to solve real problems.

subject: Math
description: Use tiling to show in a concrete case that the area of a rectangle with whole-number side lengths a and b + c is the sum of a x b and a x c. Use area models to represent the distributive property in mathematical reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can use small squares to show that the area of a rectangle with sides a and b + c is the same as adding the areas of rectangles with sides a and b, and a and c.

subject: Math
description: Multiply side lengths to find areas of rectangles with whole-number side lengths in the context of solving real world and mathematical problems, and represent whole-number products as rectangular areas in mathematical reasoning.
description_plain: Your child multiplies side lengths to find the area of rectangles and uses this to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of a rectangle with whole-number side lengths by tiling it, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths.
description_plain: Your child finds the area of a rectangle by counting squares or multiplying side lengths.

subject: Math
description: Relate area to the operations of multiplication and addition.
description_plain: Your child understands that area relates to multiplication and addition.

subject: Math
description: Measure areas by counting unit squares (square cm, square m, square in., square ft., and improvised units).
description_plain: Your child can find the area of shapes by counting how many square units fit inside.

subject: Math
description: A plane figure which can be covered without gaps or overlaps by n unit squares is said to have an area of n square units.
description_plain: Your child knows that if a shape can be covered exactly by n squares, its area is n square units.

subject: Math
description: A square with side length 1 unit, called "a unit square," is said to have "one square unit" of area, and can be used to measure area.
description_plain: Your child understands that a square with sides one unit long is called a unit square and measures one square unit of area.

subject: Math
description: Recognize area as an attribute of plane figures and understand concept of area measurement.
description_plain: Your child understands that area is a way to measure flat shapes.

subject: Math
description: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of area and relate area to multiplication and to addition.
description_plain: Your child understands what area means and how it connects to multiplication and addition.

subject: Math
description: Generate measurement data by measuring lengths using rulers marked with halves and fourths of an inch. Show the data by making a line plot where the horizontal scale is marked off in appropriate units – whole numbers, halves, or quarters.
description_plain: Your child measures lengths using rulers marked in halves and quarters of an inch and shows the results on a line plot.

subject: Math
description: Draw a scaled picture graph and a scaled bar graph to represent a data set with several categories. Solve one- and two-step "how many more" and "how may less" problems using information presented in scaled bar graphs.
description_plain: Your child can draw picture and bar graphs with scales to show data and solve questions about how much more or less one category has compared to another.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Measure and estimate liquid volumes and masses of objects using standard units of grams (g), kilograms kg), and liters (l). Add, subtract, multiply, or divide to solve one-step word problems involving masses or volumes that are given in the same units, e.g., by using drawings (such as a beaker with a measurement scale) to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can measure and estimate how much liquids and objects weigh or hold using grams, kilograms, and liters, and solve simple word problems using drawings.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time to the nearest minute and measure time intervals in minutes. Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of time intervals in minutes, e.g., by representing the problem on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the nearest minute, measure time intervals, and solve problems about adding or subtracting time using number lines.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving measurement and estimation of intervals of time, liquid volumes, and masses of objects.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems involving measuring and estimating time, liquid amounts, and weights.

subject: Math
description: Compare two fractions with the same numerator or the same denominator by reasoning about their size. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two fractions refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, or <, and justify the conclusions, e.g., by using a visual fraction model.
description_plain: Your child can compare two fractions with the same top number or the same bottom number by thinking about their size and explain the comparison using >, =, or < and pictures.

subject: Math
description: Express whole numbers as fractions, and recognize fractions that are equivalent to whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can write whole numbers as fractions and recognize when fractions equal whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and generate simple equivalent fractions, e.g., ½ = 2/4, 4/6 = 2/3). Explain why the fractions are equivalent, e.g., by using a visual fraction model
description_plain: Your child can find and explain simple fractions that are equal, like 1/2 = 2/4, using pictures.

subject: Math
description: Represent two fractions as equivalent (equal) if they are the same size, or the same point on the number line.
description_plain: Your child can show that two fractions are equal if they represent the same amount or point on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Explain equivalence of fractions in special cases, and compare fractions by reasoning about their size.
description_plain: Your child can explain when fractions are equal and compare their sizes.

subject: Math
description: Represent a fraction a/b on a number line diagram by marking off a lengths of 1/b from 0. Recognize that the resulting interval has size a/b and that its endpoint locates the number a/b on the number line.
description_plain: Your child can show a fraction a/b on a number line by marking off a parts of size 1/b from zero and understand where a/b is located.

subject: Math
description: Represent a fraction 1/b on a number line diagram by defining the interval from 0 to 1 as the whole and partitioning it into b equal parts. Recognize that each part has size 1/b and that the endpoint of the part based at 0 locates the number 1/b on the number line.
description_plain: Your child can show the fraction 1/b on a number line by dividing the space from 0 to 1 into equal parts and identifying one part.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction as a number on the number line; represent fractions on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child understands fractions as numbers and can place them correctly on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction 1/b as the quantity formed by 1 part when a whole is partitioned into b equal parts; understand a fraction a/b as the quantity formed by a parts of size 1/b.
description_plain: Your child understands that 1/b means one part when a whole is split into b equal parts, and a/b means a parts of size 1/b.

subject: Math
description: Develop understanding of fractions as numbers.
description_plain: Your child learns that fractions are numbers and how they work.

subject: Math
description: Multiply one-digit whole numbers by multiples of 10 in the range of 10-90 (e.g., 9 x 80, 5 x 60) using strategies based on place value and properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can multiply one-digit numbers by tens from 10 to 90 using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 1000 using strategies and algorithms based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 1000 using strategies based on place value and math facts.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round whole numbers to the nearest 10 or 100.
description_plain: Your child can round whole numbers to the nearest 10 or 100 using place value.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operation to perform multi-digit arithmetic.
description_plain: Your child can use place value and math properties to do multi-digit addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.

subject: Math
description: Identify arithmetic patterns (including patterns in the addition table or multiplication table), and explain them using properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can find patterns in addition and multiplication and explain them using math rules.

subject: Math
description: Solve two-step word problems using the four operations. Represent these problems using equations with a letter standing for the unknown quantity. Assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies including rounding.
description_plain: Your child can solve two-step word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division and check their answers by estimating.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving the four operations, and identify and explain patterns in arithmetic.
description_plain: Your child can solve math problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division, and can find and explain patterns in numbers.

subject: Math
description: Fluently multiply and divide within 100, using strategies such as the relationship between multiplication and division (e.g., knowing that 8 x 5 = 40, one knows 40 ÷ 5 = 8) or properties of operations. By the end of Grade 3, know from memory all products of two one-digit numbers.
description_plain: Your child can quickly multiply and divide numbers up to 100 using strategies like knowing related facts and math properties, and knows all multiplication and division facts for single-digit numbers by memory.

subject: Math
description: Multiply and divide within 100.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Understand division as an unknown-factor problem.
description_plain: Your child understands division as finding an unknown number that, when multiplied, gives the original number.

subject: Math
description:
Apply properties of operations as strategies to multiply and divide.
  • If 6 x 4 = 24 is known, then 4 x 6 = 24 is also known. (Commutative property of multiplication)
  • 3 x 5 x 2 can be found by 3 x 5 = 15, then 15 x 2 = 30, or by 5 x 2 = 10, then 3 x 10 = 30. (Associative property of multiplication)
  • Knowing that 8 x 5 = 40 and 8 x 2 = 16, one can find 8 x 7 as 8 x (5 + 2) = (8 x 5) + (8 x 2) which leads to 40 + 16 = 56. (Distributive property)
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to multiply and divide, like switching factors, grouping numbers differently, and breaking numbers apart to multiply.

subject: Math
description: Understand properties of multiplication and the relationship between multiplication and division.
description_plain: Your child understands how multiplication works and how it is connected to division.

subject: Math
description: Determine the unknown whole number in a multiplication or division equation relating three whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can find the missing number in multiplication or division problems without needing special math words.

subject: Math
description: Use multiplication and division within 100 to solve word problems in situations involving equal groups, arrays, and measurement quantities, e.g., by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems about equal groups, arrays, or measurements using multiplication and division, and write equations with a letter for the unknown.

subject: Math
description: Interpret whole-number quotients of whole numbers, e.g., interpret 56 ÷ 8 as the number of objects in each share when 56 objects are partitioned equally into 8 shares, or as a number of shares when 56 objects are partitioned into equal shares of 8 objects each.
description_plain: Your child can explain division problems like 56 ÷ 8 by showing how many objects are in each group or how many groups there are.

subject: Math
description: Interpret products of whole numbers, e.g., interpret 5 x 7 as the total number of objects in 5 groups of 7 objects each.
description_plain: Your child can understand multiplication as finding the total number of objects in equal groups, like 5 groups of 7 objects.

subject: Math
description: Represent and solve problems involving multiplication and division.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems using multiplication and division.

subject: Math
description: Partition circles and rectangles into two, three, or four equal shares, describe the shares using the words halves, thirds, half of, a third of, etc., and describe the whole as two halves, three thirds, four fourths. Recognize that equal shares of identical wholes need not have the same shape.
description_plain: Your child can divide circles and rectangles into equal parts like halves, thirds, or fourths and describe these parts and the whole.

subject: Math
description: Partition a rectangle into rows and columns of same-size squares and count to find the total number of them.
description_plain: Your child can divide a rectangle into equal squares by making rows and columns and count how many squares there are.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and draw shapes having specific attributes, such as a given number of angles or a given number of equal faces. Identify triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, and cubes.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and draw shapes by their features, like how many angles or equal sides they have, and name shapes like triangles and cubes.

subject: Math
description: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
description_plain: Your child understands shapes and their features and can think about how they are alike and different.

subject: Math
description: Draw a picture graph and a bar graph (with single-unit scale) to represent a data set with up to four categories. Solve simple put-together, take-apart, and compare problems using information presented in a bar graph.
description_plain: Your child can make picture and bar graphs with up to four categories and use them to solve simple problems.

subject: Math
description: Generate measurement data by measuring lengths of several objects to the nearest whole unit, or by making repeated measurements of the same object. Show the measurements by making a line plot, where the horizontal scale is marked off in whole-number units.
description_plain: Your child can measure several objects, record the lengths on a line plot, and organize the data.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving dollar bills, quarters, dimes, nickels, and pennies, using $ and ¢ symbols appropriately.
description_plain: Your child can solve money problems using coins and dollar bills and write amounts with $ and ¢ symbols.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time from analog and digital clocks to the nearest five minutes, using a.m. and p.m.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the nearest five minutes on analog and digital clocks, using a.m. and p.m.

subject: Math
description: Work with time and money.
description_plain: Work with time and money.

subject: Math
description: Represent whole numbers as lengths from 0 on a number line diagram with equally spaced points corresponding to the number 0, 1, 2, …, and represent whole-number sums and differences within 100 on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can show numbers on a number line and use it to add and subtract numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve word problems involving lengths that are given in the same units, e.g., by using drawings (such as drawings of rulers) and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems about lengths using addition and subtraction within 100, with drawings or equations.

subject: Math
description: Relate addition and subtraction to length.
description_plain: Your child understands how addition and subtraction relate to measuring length.

subject: Math
description: Measure to determine how much longer one object is than another, expressing the length difference in terms of a standard length unit.
description_plain: Your child can measure how much longer one object is than another using standard units.

subject: Math
description: Estimate lengths using units of inches, feet, centimeters, and meters.
description_plain: Your child can estimate lengths using inches, feet, centimeters, and meters.

subject: Math
description: Measure the length of an object twice, using length units of different lengths for the two measurements; describe how the two measurements relate to the size of the unit chosen.
description_plain: Your child can measure something twice using different units and explain how the measurements change with the unit size.

subject: Math
description: Measure the length of an object by selecting and using appropriate tools such as rulers, yardsticks, meter sticks, and measuring tapes.
description_plain: Your child can measure how long things are using tools like rulers or measuring tapes.

subject: Math
description: Measure and estimate lengths in standard units.
description_plain: Your child can measure and estimate lengths using standard units.

subject: Math
description: Explain why addition and subtraction strategies work, using place value and the properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can explain why their ways of adding and subtracting work by using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Mentally add 10 or 100 to a given number 100-900, and mentally subtract 10 or 100 from a given number 100-900.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add or subtract 10 or 100 to numbers between 100 and 900 in their head.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 1000, using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method. Understand that in adding or subtracting three-digit numbers, one adds or subtracts hundreds and hundreds, tens and tens, ones and ones; and sometimes it is necessary to compose or decompose tens or hundreds.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 1000 using drawings or models and understand how to add hundreds, tens, and ones separately.

subject: Math
description: Add up to four two-digit numbers using strategies based on place value, properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can add up to four two-digit numbers using place value and math strategies.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 100 using strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers within 100 using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
description_plain: Your child can use their understanding of place value to add and subtract numbers.

subject: Math
description: Compare two three-digit numbers based on meanings of the hundreds, tens, and ones digits, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can compare two three-digit numbers by looking at the hundreds, tens, and ones, and use the symbols >, =, and < to show which is bigger or smaller.

subject: Math
description: Read and write numbers to 1000 using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form.
description_plain: Your child can read and write numbers up to 1000 in different ways, like with digits, words, or expanded form.

subject: Math
description: Count within 1000; skip-count by 5s, 10s, and 100s.
description_plain: Your child can count to 1000 and skip count by 5s, 10s, and 100s.

subject: Math
description: Understand the following as a special case: The numbers 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900 refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine hundreds (and 0 tens and 0 ones).
description_plain: Your child understands that numbers like 100, 200, 300, etc., mean one, two, three, and more hundreds with zero tens and ones.

subject: Math
description: Understand the following as a special case: 100 can be thought of as a bundle of ten tens -- called a "hundred."
description_plain: Your child understands that 100 is made of ten groups of ten, called a hundred.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the three digits of a three-digit number represent amounts of hundreds, tens, and ones; e.g., 706 equals 7 hundreds, 0 tens, and 6 ones.
description_plain: Your child understands that a three-digit number shows how many hundreds, tens, and ones it has, like 706 means 7 hundreds, 0 tens, and 6 ones.

subject: Math
description: Understand place value.
description_plain: Understand place value.

subject: Math
description: Use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rectangular arrays with up to 5 rows and up to 5 columns; write an equation to express the total as a sum of equal addends.
description_plain: Your child can use addition to find out how many objects are in a rectangle with up to 5 rows and 5 columns, and can write an equation to show this.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether a group of objects (up to 20 has an odd or even number of members, e.g., by pairing objects or counting them by 2s; write an equation to express an even number as a sum of two equal addends.
description_plain: Your child can tell if a group of up to 20 objects has an odd or even number by pairing or counting by twos and can write even numbers as two equal parts added together.

subject: Math
description: Work with equal groups of objects to gain foundations for multiplication.
description_plain: Your child works with equal groups of objects to start learning about multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 20 using mental strategies. By end of Grade 2, know from memory all sums of two one-digit numbers.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 20 in their head and knows all sums of two one-digit numbers by memory.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 20.
description_plain: Add and subtract within 20.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve one- and two-step word problems involving situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart, and comparing, with unknowns in all positions, e.g., by using drawings, and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve one- and two-step word problems using addition and subtraction within 100, drawing pictures and writing equations with unknown numbers.

subject: Math
description: Represent and solve problems involving addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can use addition and subtraction to solve different kinds of problems.

subject: Math
description: Recognize a line of symmetry for a two-dimensional figure as a line across the figure such that the figure can be folded along the line into matching parts. Identify line-symmetric figures and draw lines of symmetry.
description_plain: Your child can find and draw lines of symmetry on flat shapes by folding them to see if both sides match.

subject: Math
description: Classify two-dimensional figures based on the presence or absence of parallel or perpendicular lines, or the presence or absence of angles of a specified size. Recognize right triangles as a category, and identify right triangles.
description_plain: Your child can sort flat shapes by looking for parallel or perpendicular lines and by naming triangles as right, acute, or obtuse based on their angles.

subject: Math
description: Draw points, lines, line segments, rays, angles (right, acute, obtuse), and perpendicular and parallel lines. Identify these in two-dimensional figures.
description_plain: Your child can draw points, lines, line segments, rays, and different types of angles, and find these parts in flat shapes.

subject: Math
description: Draw and identify lines and angles, and classify shape by properties of their lines and angles.
description_plain: Your child can draw and name lines and angles and sort shapes based on their lines and angles.

subject: Math
description: Recognize angle measure as additive. When an angle is decomposed into non-overlapping parts, the angle measure of the whole is the sum of the angle measures of the parts. Solve addition and subtraction problems to find unknown angles on a diagram in real world and mathematical problems, e.g., by using an equation with a symbol for the unknown angle measure.
description_plain: Your child can find the size of a whole angle by adding the sizes of its parts and solve problems to find missing angles using addition or subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Measure angles in whole-number degrees using a protractor. Sketch angles of specified measure.
description_plain: Your child can measure angles using a protractor and draw angles with a given size.

subject: Math
description: An angle that turns through n one-degree angles is said to have an angle measure of n degrees.
description_plain: Your child knows that an angle made by turning through n one-degree steps measures n degrees.

subject: Math
description: An angle is measured with reference to a circle with its center at the common endpoint of the rays, by considering the fraction of the circular arc between the points where the two rays intersect the circle. An angle that turns through 1/360 of the circle is called a "one-degree angle," and can be used to measure angles.
description_plain: Your child understands that an angle is measured by how much it turns around a point, with one degree being 1/360 of a full turn.

subject: Math
description: Recognize angles as geometric shapes that are formed wherever two rays share a common endpoint, and understand concepts of angle measurement.
description_plain: Your child knows that angles are shapes made by two rays sharing a point and learns how to measure them.

subject: Math
description: Geometric measurement: understand concepts or angle and measure angles.
description_plain: Your child understands what angles are and can measure them.

subject: Math
description: Make a line plot to display a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit (½, ¼, 1/8). Solve problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions by using information presented in line plots.
description_plain: Your child can make a line plot to show measurements using fractions like 1/2 or 1/4 and solve addition or subtraction problems using the plot.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Apply the area and perimeter formulas for rectangles in real world and mathematical problems. For example, find the width of a rectangular room given the area of the flooring and the length, by viewing the area formulas as a multiplication equation with an unknown factor.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas to find the area and perimeter of rectangles and solve real problems, like finding a room’s width when given area and length.

subject: Math
description: Use the four operations to solve word problems involving distances, intervals of time, liquid volumes, masses of objects, and money, including problems involving simple fractions or decimals, and problems that require expressing measurements given in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit. Represent measurement quantities using diagrams such as number line diagrams that feature a measurement scale.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems involving distances, time, volume, weight, and money using addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division, including problems with fractions or decimals, and can show measurements on number lines.

subject: Math
description: Know relative sizes of measurement units within one system of units including km, m, cm, kg, g; lb, oz.; l, ml; hr, min, sec. Within a single system of measurement, express measurements in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit. Record measurement equivalents in a two-column table.
description_plain: Your child knows how big different units are in one measurement system, like kilometers and meters or pounds and ounces, and can write bigger units as smaller units in a table.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving measurement and conversion of measurements for a larger unit to a smaller unit.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems that involve changing measurements from bigger units to smaller units.

subject: Math
description: Compare two decimals to hundredths by reasoning about their size. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two decimals refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, <, and justify the conclusions, e.g., by using a visual model.
description_plain: Your child can compare two decimals to hundredths by thinking about their size, use >, =, or < to show which is bigger, and explain why, making sure the decimals refer to the same whole.

subject: Math
description: Use decimal notation for fractions with denominators 10 and 100.
description_plain: Your child can write fractions with denominators 10 or 100 as decimal numbers.

subject: Math
description: Express a fraction with denominator 10 as an equivalent fraction with denominator 100, and use this technique to add two fractions with respective denominators 10 and 100.
description_plain: Your child can change a fraction with denominator 10 into an equal fraction with denominator 100 and use this to add fractions with denominators 10 and 100.

subject: Math
description: Understand decimal notation for fractions, and compare decimal fractions.
description_plain: Your child can understand how fractions and decimals are related and can compare decimal numbers.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving multiplication of a fraction by a whole number, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that multiply a fraction by a whole number using pictures and equations.

subject: Math
description: Understand a multiple of a/b as a multiple of 1/b, and use this understanding to multiply a fraction by a whole number.
description_plain: Your child understands that multiplying a fraction by a whole number means multiplying the numerator and can use models to show this.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction a/b as a multiple of 1/b.
description_plain: Your child understands a fraction like a/b as a number made up of a parts of size 1/b.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction by a whole number.
description_plain: Your child can multiply a fraction by a whole number and solve real-life problems using this skill.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole and having like denominators, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that add or subtract fractions with the same denominator using drawings or fraction models.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract mixed numbers with like denominators, e.g., by replacing each mixed number with an equivalent fraction, and/or by using properties of operations and the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract mixed numbers with the same bottom number by changing them into improper fractions or using addition and subtraction rules.

subject: Math
description: Decompose a fraction into a sum of fractions with the same denominator in more than one way, recording each decomposition as an equation. Justify decompositions, e.g., by using a visual fraction model.
description_plain: Your child can break a fraction into smaller fractions with the same bottom number in different ways and explain their thinking using drawings or models.

subject: Math
description: Understand addition and subtraction of fractions as joining and separating parts referring to the same whole.
description_plain: Your child understands that adding and subtracting fractions means putting parts together or taking parts apart from the same whole.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction a/b with a >1 as a sum of fractions 1/b.
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction like a/b (when a is more than 1) is the same as adding 1/b fractions a times.

subject: Math
description: Build fractions from unit fractions by applying and extending previous understandings of operations on whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can build fractions by adding unit fractions and use what they know about whole numbers to do this.

subject: Math
description: Compare two fractions with different numerators and different denominators, e.g., by creating common denominators or numerators, or by comparing to a benchmark fraction such as ½. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two fractions refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with symbols >, =, <, and justify the conclusions, e.g., by using a visual fraction model.
description_plain: Your child can compare two fractions with different top and bottom numbers by finding common denominators or numerators, or by comparing to ½, and can use >, =, or < to show which is bigger, explaining their answer with pictures.

subject: Math
description: Explain why a fraction a/b is equivalent to a fraction (n x a)/(n x b) by using visual fraction models, with attention to how the number and size of the parts differ even though the two fractions themselves are the same size. Use this principle to recognize and generate equivalent fractions.
description_plain: Your child can explain why multiplying the top and bottom of a fraction by the same number makes an equal fraction, using pictures to show the parts.

subject: Math
description: Extend understanding of fraction equivalence and ordering.
description_plain: Your child understands how to tell if two fractions are equal and can put fractions in order from smallest to largest.

subject: Math
description: Find whole-number quotients and remainders with up to four-digit dividends and one-digit divisors, using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can divide numbers up to four digits by one-digit numbers, find the quotient and remainder, and explain their work using equations and pictures like arrays or area models.

subject: Math
description: Multiply a whole number of up to four digits by a one-digit whole number, and multiply two two- digit numbers, using strategies based on place value and the properties of operations. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can multiply numbers up to four digits by one-digit numbers and multiply two two-digit numbers using place value and math rules, and explain their work with pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract multi-digit whole numbers using the standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract large whole numbers using the standard method.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to perform multi-digit arithmetic.
description_plain: Your child can use their understanding of place value to solve problems with large numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round multi- digit whole numbers to any place.
description_plain: Your child can round multi-digit numbers to any place value using their understanding of place value.

subject: Math
description: Read and write multi-digit whole numbers using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form. Compare two multi-digit numbers based on meanings of the digits in each place, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can read and write large whole numbers in different ways and compare two numbers by looking at the value of each digit, using >, =, or < to show which is bigger.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that in a multi-digit whole number, a digit in one place represents ten times what it represents in the place to its right.
description_plain: Your child understands that in a big number, each digit is worth ten times more than the digit to its right.

subject: Math
description: Generalize place value understanding for multi-digit whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child understands how place value works for numbers with more than one digit.

subject: Math
description: Generate a number or shape pattern that follows a given rule. Identify apparent features of the pattern that were not explicit in the rule itself. For example, given the rule "Add 3" and the starting number 1, generate terms in the resulting sequence and observe that the terms appear to alternate between odd and even numbers. Explain informally why the numbers will continue to alternate in this way.
description_plain: Your child can create number or shape patterns that follow a rule and notice new features in the pattern that weren’t obvious from the rule.

subject: Math
description: Generate and analyze patterns.
description_plain: Your child can create and study patterns in numbers or shapes.

subject: Math
description: Find all factor pairs for a whole number in the range 1-100.Recognize that a whole number is a multiple of each of its factors. Determine whether a given whole number in the range 1- 100 is a multiple of a given one-digit number. Determine whether a given whole number in the range of 1-100 is prime or composite.
description_plain: Your child can find all pairs of numbers that multiply to a number between 1 and 100, know that a number is a multiple of its factors, check if a number is a multiple of a one-digit number, and decide if a number is prime or composite.

subject: Math
description: Gain familiarity with factors and multiples.
description_plain: Your child is getting to know how factors and multiples work.

subject: Math
description: Solve multistep word problems posed with whole numbers and having whole-number answers using the four operations, including problems in which remainders must be interpreted. Represent these problems using equations with a letter standing for the unknown quantity. Assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies including rounding.
description_plain: Your child can solve multi-step word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division, including problems with remainders, and write equations with letters for unknowns, checking if answers make sense by estimating.

subject: Math
description: Multiply or divide to solve word problems involving multiplicative comparison, e.g., by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem, distinguishing multiplicative comparison from additive comparison.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that compare amounts by multiplying or dividing, using drawings and equations with symbols for unknown numbers.

subject: Math
description: Interpret a multiplication equation as a comparison, e.g., interpret 35 = 5 x 7 as a statement that 35 is 5 times as many as 7 and 7 times as many as 5. Represent verbal statements of multiplicative comparisons as multiplication equations.
description_plain: Your child can understand multiplication as a way to compare numbers, like knowing 35 is 5 times 7, and can write multiplication equations from words that describe these comparisons.

subject: Math
description: Use the four operations with whole numbers to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child can use addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division to solve different kinds of problems.

subject: Math
description: Partition circles and rectangles into two and four equal shares, describe the shares using the words halves, fourths, and quarters, and use the phrases half of, fourth of, and quarter of. Describe the whole as two of, or four of the shares. Understand for these examples that decomposing into more equal shares creates smaller shares.
description_plain: Your child can divide circles and rectangles into two or four equal parts and use words like halves and quarters to describe them.

subject: Math
description: Compose two-dimensional shapes (rectangles, squares, trapezoids, triangles, half-circles, and quarter-circles) or three-dimensional shapes (cubes, right rectangular prisms, right circular cones, and right circular cylinders) to create a composite shape, and compose new shapes from the composite shape.
description_plain: Your child can put together flat shapes or solid shapes to make new shapes and can also break apart shapes to make others.

subject: Math
description: Distinguish between defining attributes (e.g., triangles are closed and three-sided) versus non-defining attributes (e.g., color, orientation, overall size); build and draw shapes to possess defining attributes.
description_plain: Your child can tell the important features that define shapes, like a triangle having three sides, and can build and draw shapes with those features.

subject: Math
description: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
description_plain: Your child can think about shapes and their features.

subject: Math
description: Organize, represent, and interpret data with up to three categories; ask and answer questions about the total number of data points, how many in each category, and how many more or less are in one category than in another.
description_plain: Your child can collect and organize data into up to three groups and ask and answer questions about how many are in each group and how the groups compare.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time in hours and half-hours using analog and digital clocks.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the hour and half-hour using both analog and digital clocks.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time.
description_plain: Tell and write time.

subject: Math
description: Express the length of an object as a whole number of length units, by laying multiple copies of a shorter object (the length unit) end to end; understand that the length measurement of an object is the number of same-size length units that span it with no gaps or overlaps. Limit to contexts where the object being measured is spanned by a whole number of length units with no gaps or overlaps.
description_plain: Your child can measure how long something is by lining up smaller, same-sized objects end to end without gaps or overlaps.

subject: Math
description: Measure lengths indirectly and by iterating length unit.
description_plain: Your child can measure lengths by using repeated units or by measuring indirectly.

subject: Math
description: Subtract multiples of 10 in the range of 10-90 from multiples of 10 in the range of 10-90 (positive or zero differences), using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can subtract multiples of 10 between 10 and 90 using drawings or objects, explain how they did it, and connect it to written subtraction methods.

subject: Math
description: Given a two-digit number, mentally find 10 more or 10 less than the number, without having to count; explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can quickly find 10 more or 10 less than a two-digit number in their head and explain how they know.

subject: Math
description: Add within 100, including adding a two-digit number and a one-digit number, and adding a two-digit number and a multiple of 10, using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used. Understand that in adding two-digit numbers, one adds tens and tens, ones and ones, and sometimes it is necessary to compose a ten.
description_plain: Your child can add numbers up to 100, including adding two-digit and one-digit numbers or multiples of 10, using objects or drawings and explain their thinking.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
description_plain: Your child can use their understanding of tens and ones to add and subtract numbers.

subject: Math
description: Compare two two-digit numbers based on meanings of the tens and ones digits, recording the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, and <.
description_plain: Your child can compare two two-digit numbers by looking at the tens and ones, and use symbols like >, =, and < to show which is bigger or smaller.

subject: Math
description: Understand the following as a special case: The numbers 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine tens (and 0 ones).
description_plain: Your child understands that numbers like 10, 20, 30, up to 90 mean one to nine groups of ten, with zero ones.

subject: Math
description: Understand the following as a special case: The numbers from 11 to 19 are composed of a ten and one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine ones.
description_plain: Your child knows that numbers from 11 to 19 are made of one ten plus some ones.

subject: Math
description: Understand the following as a special case: 10 can be thought of as a bundle of ten ones -- called a "ten."
description_plain: Your child understands that 10 is a group of ten ones, called a 'ten.'

subject: Math
description: Understand that the two digits of a two-digit number represent amounts of tens and ones.
description_plain: Your child knows that in two-digit numbers, the first digit shows how many tens and the second digit shows how many ones.

subject: Math
description: Understand place value.
description_plain: Understand place value.

subject: Math
description: Count to 120 starting at any number less than 120. In this range, read and write numerals and represent a number of objects with a written numeral.
description_plain: Your child can count up to 120 starting from any number less than 120, read and write numbers, and match numbers to groups of objects.

subject: Math
description: Extend the counting sequence.
description_plain: Extend the counting sequence.

subject: Math
description: Determine the unknown whole number in an addition or subtraction equation relating three whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can figure out the missing number in an addition or subtraction problem with three numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand the meaning of the equal sign, and determine if equations involving addition and subtraction are true or false.
description_plain: Your child understands what the equal sign means and can tell if addition or subtraction equations are true or false.

subject: Math
description: Work with addition and subtraction equations.
description_plain: Your child can work with addition and subtraction equations.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 20, demonstrating fluency for addition and subtraction within 10. Use strategies such as counting on, making ten (e.g. 8 + 6 = 8 + 2 + 4, which leads to 10 + 4 = 14); decomposing a number leading to a ten (e.g., 13 – 4 = 13 – 3 – 1, which leads to 10 – 1 = 9); using the relationship between addition and subtraction (e.g., knowing that 8 + 4 = 12, one knows 12 – 8 = 4); and creating equivalent but easier or known sums (e.g., adding 6 + 7 by creating the known equivalent 6 + 6 + 1 = 12 + 1, which equals 13).
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 20 and quickly add and subtract numbers up to 10 using helpful tricks like making ten or breaking numbers apart.

subject: Math
description: Relate counting to addition and subtraction (e.g., by counting on 2 to add 2).
description_plain: Your child can use counting to help add and subtract numbers, like counting on to add.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 20.
description_plain: Add and subtract within 20.

subject: Math
description: Understand subtraction as an unknown-addend problem. For example, subtract 10 – 8 by finding the number that makes 10 when added to 8.
description_plain: Your child can solve subtraction problems by thinking about what number added to the smaller number equals the bigger number.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add and subtract. (Students need not use formal terms for these properties.)
description_plain: Your child uses simple rules about adding and subtracting to help solve problems, even if they don’t know the formal names.

subject: Math
description: Understand and apply properties of operations and relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child understands how addition and subtraction are connected and can use properties of these operations to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems that call for addition of three whole numbers whose sum is less than or equal to 20, e.g., by using objects, drawings, and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that add three numbers with a total of 20 or less using objects, drawings, or equations with a missing number.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 20 to solve word problems involving situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart, and comparing, with unknowns in all positions, e.g., by using objects, drawings, and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems with addition and subtraction within 20 by using objects, drawings, or simple equations with a blank for the unknown number.

subject: Math
description: Represent and solve problems involving addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can show and solve problems using addition and subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Compose simple shapes to form larger shapes.
description_plain: Your child can put simple shapes together to make bigger shapes.

subject: Math
description: Model shapes in the world by building shapes from components (e.g., sticks and clay balls) and drawing shapes.
description_plain: Your child can build and draw shapes they see around them using parts like sticks and clay balls.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and compare two- and three-dimensional shapes, in different sizes and orientations, using informal language to describe their similarities, differences, parts (e.g., number of sides and vertices/"corners") and other attributes (e.g., having sides of equal length).
description_plain: Your child can look at flat and solid shapes of different sizes and talk about how they are alike and different, including counting sides and corners.

subject: Math
description: Analyze, compare, create, and compose shape.
description_plain: Your child can look at, compare, make, and put together different shapes.

subject: Math
description: Identify shapes as two-dimensional (lying in a plane, "flat") or three-dimensional ("solid").
description_plain: Your child can tell if a shape is flat (2D) or solid (3D).

subject: Math
description: Correctly name shapes regardless of their orientations or overall size.
description_plain: Your child can correctly name shapes no matter how they are turned or how big they are.

subject: Math
description: Describe objects in the environment using names of shapes, and describe the relative positions of these objects using terms such as above, below, beside, in front of, behind, and next to.
description_plain: Your child can name shapes they see around them and describe where things are using words like above, below, beside, in front of, behind, and next to.

subject: Math
description: Identify and describe shapes (squares, circles, triangles, rectangles, hexagons, cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres).
description_plain: Your child can identify and talk about shapes like squares, circles, triangles, rectangles, hexagons, cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres.

subject: Math
description: Classify objects into given categories; count the number of objects in each category and sort the categories by count. (Limit category counts to be less than or equal to 10.).
description_plain: Your child can sort objects into groups, count how many are in each group, and arrange the groups by size, with counts up to 10.

subject: Math
description: Classify objects and count the number of objects in each category.
description_plain: Your child can sort objects into groups and count how many are in each group.

subject: Math
description: Directly compare two objects with a measurable attribute in common, to see which object has "more of"/"less of" the attribute, and describe the difference.
description_plain: Your child can compare two objects to see which one is longer, heavier, or has more of something and describe the difference.

subject: Math
description: Describe measurable attributes of objects, such as length or weight. Describe several measurable attributes of a single object.
description_plain: Your child can describe things you can measure about objects, like how long or heavy they are, and describe several of these for one object.

subject: Math
description: Describe and compare measurable attributes.
description_plain: Your child can describe and compare things that can be measured.

subject: Math
description: Compose and decompose numbers from 11 to 19 into ten ones and some further ones, e.g., by using objects or drawings, and record each composition or decomposition by a drawing or equation (such as 18 = 10 + 8); understand that these numbers are composed of ten ones and one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine ones.
description_plain: Your child can break apart numbers from 11 to 19 into ten ones and some extra ones using objects or drawings and write it down.

subject: Math
description: Work with numbers 11-19 to gain foundations for place value.
description_plain: Your child can work with numbers 11 to 19 to start learning about place value.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 5.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 5.

subject: Math
description: For any number from 1 to 9, find the number that makes 10 when added to the given number, e.g., by using objects or drawings and record the answer with a drawing or equation.
