description_plain: Your child can find the number that adds to another number to make 10, using objects or drawings, and write the answer.

subject: Math
description: Decompose numbers less than or equal to 10 into pairs in more than one way, e.g., by using objects or drawing, and record each decomposition by a drawing or equation (e.g., 5 = 2 + 3 and 5 = 4 + 1).
description_plain: Your child can break numbers up to 10 into pairs in different ways using objects or drawings and show each way with a drawing or equation.

subject: Math
description: Solve addition and subtraction word problems, and add and subtract within 10, e.g., by using objects or drawings to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve simple addition and subtraction story problems and add or subtract numbers up to 10 using objects or drawings.

subject: Math
description: Represent addition and subtraction with objects, fingers, mental images, drawings, sounds (e.g., claps), acting out situations, or verbal explanations, expressions, or equations.
description_plain: Your child can show addition and subtraction using objects, fingers, drawings, sounds, acting, or words.

subject: Math
description: Understand addition as putting together and adding to, and understand subtraction as taking apart and taking from.
description_plain: Your child understands that addition means putting things together and subtraction means taking things apart or away.

subject: Math
description: Compare two numbers between 1 and 10 presented as written numerals.
description_plain: Your child can compare two numbers from 1 to 10 written as numerals to see which is bigger or smaller.

subject: Math
description: Identify whether the number of objects in one group is greater than, less than, or equal to the number of objects in another group, e.g., by using matching and counting strategies (Include groups with up to ten objects).
description_plain: Your child can tell if one group of up to 10 objects has more, less, or the same number as another group by matching or counting.

subject: Math
description: Compare numbers.
description_plain: Compare numbers.

subject: Math
description: Count to answer "how many?" questions about as many as 20 things arranged in a line, a rectangular array, or a circle, or as many as 10 things in a scattered configuration; given a number from 1-20, count out that many objects.
description_plain: Your child can count up to 20 objects arranged in a line, rectangle, or circle, and up to 10 objects scattered, and can count out a given number of objects up to 20.

subject: Math
description: Understand that each successive number name refers to a quantity that is one larger.
description_plain: Your child understands that when you say the next number in counting, it means one more than the number before.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the last number name said tells the number of objects counted. The number of objects is the same regardless of their arrangement or the order in which they were counted.
description_plain: Your child knows that the last number said when counting tells how many objects there are, no matter how they are arranged or counted.

subject: Math
description: When counting objects, say the number names in the standard order, pairing each object with one and only one number name and each number name with one and only one object.
description_plain: Your child can count objects by saying number names in order, matching one number to each object.

subject: Math
description: Understand the relationship between numbers and quantities; connect counting to cardinality.
description_plain: Your child understands how numbers and counting relate to the amount of things.

subject: Math
description: Count to tell the number of objects.
description_plain: Your child can count objects to find out how many there are.

subject: Math
description: Write numbers from 0 to 20. Represent a number of objects with a written numeral 0-20 (with 0 representing a count of no objects).
description_plain: Your child can write numbers from 0 to 20 and use those numbers to show how many objects there are, including zero for no objects.

subject: Math
description: Count forward beginning from a given number within the known sequence (instead of having to begin at 1).
description_plain: Your child can count forward starting from any number they know, not just from 1.

subject: Math
description: Count to 100 by ones and by tens.
description_plain: Your child can count to 100 by ones and by tens.

subject: Math
description: Know number names and the count sequence.
description_plain: Your child knows number names and the order of counting.

subject: Math
description: Understand that patterns of association can also be seen in bivariate categorical data by displaying frequencies and relative frequencies in a two-way table. Construct and interpret a two-way table summarizing data on two categorical variables collected from the same subjects. Use relative frequencies calculated for rows or columns to describe possible association between the two variables.
description_plain: Your child can make and read two-way tables that show how two categories relate and use these tables to describe possible connections.

subject: Math
description: Use the equation of a linear model to solve problems in the context of bivariate measurement data, interpreting the slope and intercept.
description_plain: Your child can use a linear equation to solve problems with two related measurements and understand what the slope and intercept mean.

subject: Math
description: Know that straight lines are widely used to model relationships between two quantitative variables. For scatter plots that suggest a linear association, informally fit a straight line, and informally assess the model fit by judging the closeness of the data points to the line.
description_plain: Your child knows that straight lines can show relationships between two numbers and can draw a line that fits data points on a scatter plot.

subject: Math
description: Construct and interpret scatter plots for bivariate measurement data to investigate patterns of association between two quantities. Describe patterns such as clustering, outliers, positive or negative association, linear association, and nonlinear association.
description_plain: Your child can make and read scatter plots to find patterns like groups, outliers, or whether two things increase or decrease together.

subject: Math
description: Investigate patterns of association in bivariate data.
description_plain: Your child can explore and understand patterns between two sets of data.

subject: Math
description: Know the formulas for the volumes of cones, cylinders, and spheres and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child knows formulas to find the volume of cones, cylinders, and spheres and can use them to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving volume of cylinders, cones, and spheres.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems involving the volume of cylinders, cones, and spheres.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points in a coordinate system.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points on a coordinate plane.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to determine unknown side lengths in right triangles in real-world and mathematical problems in two and three dimensions.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find missing side lengths in right triangles in both 2D and 3D problems.

subject: Math
description: Explain a proof of the Pythagorean Theorem and its converse.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the Pythagorean Theorem works and also explain its reverse.

subject: Math
description: Use informal arguments to establish facts about the angle sum and exterior angle of triangles, about the angles created when parallel lines are cut by a transversal, and the angle- angle criterion for similarity of triangles.
description_plain: Your child can explain facts about triangle angles, angles made by parallel lines, and how to tell if triangles are similar.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a two-dimensional figure is similar to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of rotations, reflections, translations, and dilations; given two similar two-dimensional figures, describe a sequence that exhibits the similarity between them.
description_plain: Your child understands that two shapes are similar if one can be changed into the other by moving, flipping, or resizing, and can describe how to do this.

subject: Math
description: Describe the effect of dilations, translations, rotations, and reflections on two-dimensional figures using coordinates.
description_plain: Your child can describe how moving, flipping, turning, and resizing shapes changes their coordinates.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a two-dimensional figure is congruent to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of rotations, reflections, and translations; given two congruent figures, describe a sequence that exhibits the congruence between them.
description_plain: Your child understands that two shapes are congruent if one can be moved or flipped to match the other exactly, and can explain how.

subject: Math
description: Parallel lines are taken to parallel lines.
description_plain: Your child understands that parallel lines stay parallel after transformations.

subject: Math
description: Angles are taken to angles of the same measure.
description_plain: Your child understands that angles keep their size after transformations.

subject: Math
description: Lines are taken to lines, and line segments to line segments of the same length.
description_plain: Your child understands that lines and line segments keep their length after transformations.

subject: Math
description: Verify experimentally the properties of rotations, reflections, and translations.
description_plain: Your child can test and confirm how shapes change when they are rotated, flipped, or slid.

subject: Math
description: Understand congruence and similarity using physical models, transparencies, or geometry software.
description_plain: Your child understands when shapes are the same size and shape (congruent) or have the same shape but different sizes (similar) by using models or tools.

subject: Math
description: Describe qualitatively the functional relationship between two quantities by analyzing a graph (e.g., where the function is increasing or decreasing, linear or nonlinear). Sketch a graph that exhibits the qualitative features of a function that has been described verbally.
description_plain: Your child can describe how two quantities relate by looking at a graph and can draw a graph based on a verbal description of a function.

subject: Math
description: Construct a function to model a linear relationship between two quantities. Determine the rate of change and initial value of the function from a description of a relationship or from two (x, y) values, including reading these from a table or from a graph. Interpret the rate of change and initial value of a linear function in terms of the situation it models, and in terms of its graph or a table of values.
description_plain: Your child can create a function to show a straight-line relationship, find the rate of change and starting value from descriptions, tables, or graphs, and explain what they mean.

subject: Math
description: Use functions to model relationships between quantities.
description_plain: Your child can use functions to show how two quantities are related.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the equation y = mx + b as defining a linear function, whose graph is a straight line; give examples of functions that are not linear.
description_plain: Your child knows that the equation y = mx + b makes a straight line on a graph and can give examples of functions that don’t make straight lines.

subject: Math
description: Compare properties of two functions each represented in a different way (algebraically, graphically, numerically in tables, or by verbal descriptions).
description_plain: Your child compares two functions shown in different ways, like tables or equations, to decide which changes faster.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a function is a rule that assigns to each input exactly one output. The graph of a function is the set of ordered pairs consisting of an input and the corresponding output (function notation is not required in Grade 8).
description_plain: Your child understands that a function assigns one output to each input and can recognize this on a graph.

subject: Math
description: Define, evaluate, and compare functions.
description_plain: Your child can define what a function is, find its value for different inputs, and compare different functions.

subject: Math
description: Solve systems of two linear equations in two variables algebraically, and estimate solutions by graphing the equations. Solve simple cases by inspection.
description_plain: Your child can solve two linear equations with two variables using algebra and estimate solutions by graphing, including recognizing when no solution exists.

subject: Math
description: Understand that solutions to a system of two linear equations in two variables correspond to points of intersection of their graphs, because points of intersection satisfy both equations simultaneously.
description_plain: Your child understands that solutions to two linear equations are where their graphs cross because those points satisfy both equations.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and solve pairs of simultaneous linear equations.
description_plain: Your child can solve two linear equations at the same time to find where they both work.

subject: Math
description: Solve linear equations with rational number coefficients, including equations whose solutions require expanding expressions using the distributive property and collecting like terms.
description_plain: Your child can solve linear equations that have fractions or decimals by expanding and combining terms.

subject: Math
description: Give examples of linear equations in one variable with one solution, infinitely many solutions, or no solutions. Show which of these possibilities is the case by successively transforming the given equation into simpler forms, until an equivalent equation of the form x = a, a = a, or a = b results (a and b are different numbers).
description_plain: Your child can show examples of linear equations that have one solution, many solutions, or no solution by simplifying the equations step-by-step.

subject: Math
description: Solve linear equations in one variable
description_plain: Your child can solve equations with one variable, like finding the value of x.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and solve linear equations and pairs of simultaneous linear equations.
description_plain: Your child can solve single linear equations and pairs of equations with two variables.

subject: Math
description: Use similar triangles to explain why the slope m is the same between any two distinct points on a non-vertical line in the coordinate plane; derive the equation y=mx for a line through the origin, and the equation y = mx + b for a line intercepting the vertical axis at b.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the slope is the same between any two points on a line and write equations for lines that go through the origin or cross the vertical axis.

subject: Math
description: Graph proportional relationships, interpreting the unit rate as the slope of the graph. Compare two different proportional relationships represented in different ways.
description_plain: Your child can draw graphs of proportional relationships, understand the slope as the unit rate, and compare different proportional relationships.

subject: Math
description: Understand the connections between proportional relationships, lines, and linear equations.
description_plain: Your child understands how proportional relationships connect to lines and linear equations.

subject: Math
description: Perform operations with numbers expressed in scientific notation, including problems where both decimal and scientific notation are used. Use scientific notation and choose units of appropriate size for measurements of very large or very small quantities (e.g., use millimeters per year for seafloor spreading). Interpret scientific notation that has been generated by technology.
description_plain: Your child can work with very large or small numbers using scientific notation and choose the right units for measurements.

subject: Math
description: Use numbers expressed in the form of a single digit times an integer power of 10 to estimate very large or very small quantities, and to express how many times as much one is than the other.
description_plain: Your child can use numbers written as a digit times a power of 10 to estimate very big or small amounts and compare them.

subject: Math
description: Use square root and cube root symbols to represent solutions to equations of the form x² = p and x³ = p, where p is a positive rational number. Evaluate square roots of small perfect squares and cube roots of small perfect cubes. Know that √2 is irrational.
description_plain: Your child can use square root and cube root symbols to solve equations and can find roots of small perfect squares and cubes, knowing that some roots like √2 cannot be written as simple fractions.

subject: Math
description: Know and apply the properties of integer exponents to generate equivalent numerical expressions.
description_plain: Your child knows the rules for exponents with whole numbers and can use them to rewrite expressions.

subject: Math
description: Work with radicals and integer exponents.
description_plain: Your child can work with roots and whole number exponents in math problems.

subject: Math
description: Use rational approximations of irrational numbers to compare the size of irrational numbers, locate them approximately on a number line diagram, and estimate the value of expressions (e.g., π²).
description_plain: Your child can estimate and compare irrational numbers by using close rational numbers and place them roughly on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Know that numbers that are not rational are called irrational. Understand informally that every number has a decimal expansion; for rational numbers show that the decimal expansion repeats eventually, and convert a decimal expansion which repeats eventually into a rational number.
description_plain: Your child understands that irrational numbers are numbers that can't be written as fractions and can explain how decimals repeat for rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Know that there are numbers that are not rational, and approximate them by rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child knows that some numbers can’t be written as simple fractions and can estimate these numbers using fractions.

subject: Math
description: Relating the choice of measures of center and variability to the shape of the data distribution and the context in which the data was gathered.
description_plain: Your child can choose the best way to describe data based on its shape and the situation it came from.

subject: Math
description: Giving quantitative measures of center (median and/or mean) and variability (interquartile range and/or mean absolute deviation), as well as describing any overall pattern and any striking deviations from the overall pattern with reference to the context in which the data was gathered.
description_plain: Your child can describe the middle and spread of data numbers and point out any unusual values, explaining what the data is about.

subject: Math
description: Describing the nature of the attribute under investigation, including how it was measured and its units of measurement.
description_plain: Your child can describe what is being measured in data, how it was measured, and the units used.

subject: Math
description: Reporting the number of observations.
description_plain: Your child can tell how many data points or observations there are in a set.

subject: Math
description: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by:
description_plain: Your child can summarize sets of numbers by explaining what they mean in their real-life context.

subject: Math
description: Display numerical data in plots on a number line, including dot plots, histograms, and box plots.
description_plain: Your child can show number data on graphs like dot plots, histograms, and box plots using a number line.

subject: Math
description: Summarize and describe distributions.
description_plain: Your child can summarize and describe data sets.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that a measure of center for a numerical data set summarizes all of its values with a single number, while a measure of variation describes how its values vary with a single number.
description_plain: Your child knows that one number can show the center of data, and another number can show how much the data varies.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a set of data collected to answer a statistical question has a distribution which can be described by its center, spread, and overall shape.
description_plain: Your child understands that data collected to answer a question can be described by its center, spread, and overall shape.

subject: Math
description: Recognize a statistical question as one that anticipates variability in the data related to the question and accounts for it in the answers.
description_plain: Your child knows that a good statistical question expects different answers and includes that in how it is answered.

subject: Math
description: Develop understanding of statistical variability
description_plain: Your child understands that data can vary and learns how to describe that variability.

subject: Math
description: Represent three-dimensional figures using nets made up of rectangles and triangles, and use the nets to find the surface area of these figures. Apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can draw flat patterns (nets) made of rectangles and triangles to show 3D shapes and use these to find surface area in real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: Draw polygons in the coordinate plane given coordinates for the vertices; use coordinates to find the length of a side joining points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate. Apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can draw shapes on a coordinate grid using points and find side lengths when points share the same x or y value, applying this to real problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the volume of a right rectangular prism with fractional edge lengths by packing it with unit cubes of the appropriate unit fraction edge lengths, and show that the volume is the same as would be found by multiplying the edge lengths of the prism. Apply the formulas V = lwh and V = Bh to find volumes of right rectangular prisms with fractional edge lengths in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of rectangular boxes with fractional side lengths by filling them with smaller cubes and by multiplying the edges, using formulas in real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: Find area of right triangles, other triangles, special quadrilaterals, and polygons by composing into rectangles or decomposing into triangles and other shapes; apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of right triangles, other triangles, special four-sided shapes, and polygons by breaking them into rectangles or triangles, and use these skills to solve real problems.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving area, surface area, and volume.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems involving area, surface area, and volume.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent two quantities in a real-world problem that change in relationship to one another; write an equation to express one quantity, thought of as the dependent variable, in terms of the other quantity, thought of as the independent variable. Analyze the relationship between the dependent and independent variables using graphs and tables, and relate these to the equation.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to show how two changing amounts relate, write equations for them, and use graphs and tables to understand the relationship.

subject: Math
description: Represent and analyze quantitative relationships between dependent and independent variables.
description_plain: Your child can show and analyze how one quantity changes depending on another.

subject: Math
description: Write an inequality of the form x > c or x < c to represent a constraint or condition in a real-world or mathematical problem. Recognize that inequalities of the form x > c or x < chave infinitely many solutions; represent solutions of such inequalities on number line diagrams.
description_plain: Your child can write inequalities like x > c or x < c to describe limits in problems and show all possible solutions on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems by writing and solving equations of the form x +p = q and px = q for cases in which p, q and x are all nonnegative rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-world problems by writing and solving simple equations like x + p = q or px = q.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent numbers and write expressions when solving a real-world or mathematical problem; understand that a variable can represent an unknown number, or, depending on the purpose at hand, any number in a specified set.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to stand for numbers and write expressions to solve problems, knowing the letter can mean an unknown or any number in a set.

subject: Math
description: Understand solving an equation or inequality as a process of answering a question: which values from a specified set, if any, make the equation or inequality true? Use substitution to determine whether a given number in a specified set makes an equation or inequality true
description_plain: Your child understands that solving an equation or inequality means finding which numbers make it true and can check by substituting numbers.

subject: Math
description: Reason about and solve one-variable equations and inequalities.
description_plain: Your child can think through and solve equations and inequalities with one variable.

subject: Math
description: Identify when two expressions are equivalent (i.e., when the two expressions name the same number regardless of which value is substituted into them).
description_plain: Your child can tell when two math expressions are equal because they give the same answer no matter what number is used.

subject: Math
description: Apply the properties of operations to generate equivalent expressions.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to rewrite expressions that look different but mean the same thing.

subject: Math
description: Evaluate expressions at specific values for their variables. Include expressions that arise from formulas in real-world problems. Perform arithmetic operations, including those involving whole-number exponents, in the conventional order when there are no parentheses to specify a particular order (Order of Operations).
description_plain: Your child can find the value of expressions when given specific numbers, including those from real-life formulas, and follow the correct order of operations.

subject: Math
description: Identify parts of an expression using mathematical terms (sum, term, product, factor, quotient, coefficient); view one or more parts of an expression as a single entity.
description_plain: Your child can name parts of math expressions like sum, product, and factor, and see parts of an expression as one unit.

subject: Math
description: Write expressions that record operations with numbers and with letters standing for numbers.
description_plain: Your child can write math expressions using numbers and letters that stand for numbers.

subject: Math
description: Write, read, and evaluate expressions in which letters stand for numbers.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and solve math expressions that use letters to represent numbers.

subject: Math
description: Write and evaluate numerical expressions involving whole-number exponents.
description_plain: Your child can write and solve math expressions that include whole-number exponents.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of arithmetic to algebraic expressions.
description_plain: Your child can use what they know about arithmetic to work with algebra expressions.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems by graphing points in all 4 quadrants of the coordinate plane. Include use of coordinates and absolute value to find distances between points with same first coordinate or same second coordinate.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems by graphing points in all four parts of the coordinate plane and use coordinates and absolute value to find distances between points.

subject: Math
description: Distinguish comparisons of absolute value from statements about order.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between comparing absolute values and comparing the order of numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand the absolute value of a rational number as its distance from 0 on the number line; interpret absolute value as magnitude for a positive or negative quantity in a real-world situation.
description_plain: Your child understands absolute value as how far a number is from zero and can explain it in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Write, interpret, and explain statements of order for rational numbers in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child can write and explain how numbers compare in order using real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Interpret statements of inequality as statements about the relative position of two numbers on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can understand inequality statements as showing where two numbers are located on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Understand ordering and absolute value of rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child understands how to order rational numbers and what absolute value means.

subject: Math
description: Find and position integers and other rational numbers on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram; find and position pairs of integers and other rational numbers on a coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can find and place integers and rational numbers on number lines and coordinate planes.

subject: Math
description: Understand signs of numbers in ordered pairs as indicating locations in quadrants of the coordinate plane; recognize that when two ordered pairs differ only by signs, the locations of the points are related by reflections across one or both axes.
description_plain: Your child understands how the signs of numbers in pairs show locations in different parts of the coordinate plane and how changing signs reflects points across axes.

subject: Math
description: Recognize opposite signs of numbers as indicating locations on opposite sides of 0 on the number line; recognize that the opposite of the opposite of a number is the number itself, e.g., –(–3) = 3, and that 0 is its own opposite.
description_plain: Your child knows that opposite signs mean numbers are on opposite sides of zero and that the opposite of the opposite number is the original number.

subject: Math
description: Extend number line diagrams and coordinate axes familiar from previous grades to represent points on the line and in the plane with negative number coordinates.
description_plain: Your child can use number lines and coordinate planes that include negative numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand that positive and negative numbers are used together to describe quantities having opposite directions or values (e.g., temperature above/below zero, elevation above/below sea level, debits/credits, positive/negative electric charge); use positive and negative numbers to represent quantities in real-world contexts, explaining the meaning of 0 in each situation.
description_plain: Your child understands positive and negative numbers as showing opposite directions or values, like temperatures above or below zero, and can explain what zero means in these situations.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of numbers to the system of rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use what they know about numbers to understand and work with all rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Find the greatest common factor of two whole numbers less than or equal to 100 and the least common multiple of two whole numbers less than or equal to 12. Use the distributive property to express a sum of two whole numbers 1–100 with a common factor as a multiple of a sum of two whole numbers with no common factor.
description_plain: Your child can find the greatest common factor and least common multiple of numbers and use the distributive property to rewrite sums with common factors.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add, subtract, multiply, and divide multi-digit decimals using the standard algorithm for each operation.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals using standard methods.

subject: Math
description: Fluently divide multi-digit numbers using the standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can divide large numbers by multi-digit numbers using the standard method.

subject: Math
description: Compute fluently with multi-digit numbers and find common factors and multiples.
description_plain: Your child can quickly do math with large numbers and find numbers that divide evenly into others or that several numbers share.

subject: Math
description: Interpret and compute quotients of fractions, and solve word problems involving division of fractions by fractions, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can divide fractions by fractions and solve word problems about it using pictures and equations.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to divide fractions by fractions.
description_plain: Your child can divide one fraction by another using what they know about multiplication and division.

subject: Math
description: Use ratio reasoning to convert measurement units; manipulate and transform units appropriately when multiplying or dividing quantities.
description_plain: Your child can use ratio thinking to change measurement units correctly when multiplying or dividing.

subject: Math
description: Find a percent of a quantity as a rate per 100 (e.g., 30% of a quantity means 30/100 times the quantity); solve problems involving finding the whole given a part and the percent.
description_plain: Your child can find a percent of a number and solve problems where you know the part and the percent to find the whole.

subject: Math
description: Solve unit rate problems including those involving unit pricing and constant speed.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about unit rates, like price per item or speed.

subject: Math
description: Make tables of equivalent ratios relating quantities with whole-number measurements, find missing values in the tables, and plot the pairs of values on the coordinate plane. Use tables to compare ratios.
description_plain: Your child can make tables showing equal ratios with whole numbers, find missing numbers in the tables, and plot these pairs on a graph to compare ratios.

subject: Math
description: Use ratio and rate reasoning to solve real-world and mathematical problems, e.g., by reasoning about tables of equivalent ratios, tape diagrams, double number line diagrams, or equations.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems using ratios and rates, by looking at tables, diagrams, or equations.

subject: Math
description: Understand the concept of a unit rate a/b associated with a ratio a:b with b ≠ 0 (b not equal to zero), and use rate language in the context of a ratio relationship.
description_plain: Your child understands what a unit rate is and can talk about rates when comparing two amounts.

subject: Math
description: Understand the concept of a ratio and use ratio language to describe a ratio relationship between two quantities.
description_plain: Your child understands what a ratio is and can describe the relationship between two amounts using ratio language.

subject: Math
description: Understand ratio concepts and use ratio reasoning to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child understands ratios and can use them to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Design and use a simulation to generate frequencies for compound events.
description_plain: Your child can create and use simulations to find how often combined events happen.

subject: Math
description: Represent sample spaces for compound events using methods such as organized lists, tables and tree diagrams. For an event described in everyday language (e.g., "rolling double sixes"), identify the outcomes in the sample space which compose the event.
description_plain: Your child can list all possible outcomes for combined events using organized methods and identify which outcomes match a described event.

subject: Math
description: Understand that, just as with simple events, the probability of a compound event is the fraction of outcomes in the sample space for which the compound event occurs.
description_plain: Your child understands that the chance of combined events happening is the fraction of all possible outcomes that fit the event.

subject: Math
description: Find probabilities of compound events using organized lists, tables, tree diagrams, and simulation.
description_plain: Your child can find the probability of combined events using lists, tables, tree diagrams, and simulations.

subject: Math
description: Develop a probability model (which may not be uniform) by observing frequencies in data generated from a chance process.
description_plain: Your child can create a probability model by watching how often things happen in chance activities, even if outcomes are not all equally likely.

subject: Math
description: Develop a uniform probability model by assigning equal probability to all outcomes, and use the model to determine probabilities of events.
description_plain: Your child can make a probability model where all outcomes are equally likely and use it to find the chance of events.

subject: Math
description: Develop a probability model and use it to find probabilities of events; compare probabilities from a model to observed frequencies; and if the agreement is not good, explain possible sources of the discrepancy.
description_plain: Your child can create a simple probability model, use it to find chances of events, compare those chances to actual results, and explain why they might be different.

subject: Math
description: Approximate the probability of a chance event by collecting data on the chance process that produces it and observing its long-run relative frequency, and predict the approximate relative frequency given the probability.
description_plain: Your child can estimate the chance of an event by collecting data and observing how often it happens over time, then predict future chances.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the probability of a chance event is a number between 0 and 1 that expresses the likelihood of the event occurring. Larger numbers indicate greater likelihood. A probability near 0 indicates an unlikely event, a probability around ½ indicates an event that is neither unlikely nor likely, and a probability near 1 indicates a likely event.
description_plain: Your child understands that probability is a number from 0 to 1 showing how likely an event is, with numbers near 0 meaning unlikely and near 1 meaning very likely.

subject: Math
description: Investigate chance processes and develop, use, and evaluate probability models.
description_plain: Your child explores chance events and learns how to create, use, and check probability models.

subject: Math
description: Use measures of center and measures of variability for numerical data from random samples to draw informal comparative inferences about two populations.
description_plain: Your child can use averages and spread of data from random samples to make simple comparisons between two groups.

subject: Math
description: Informally assess the degree of visual overlap of two numerical data distributions with similar variabilities, measuring the difference between the centers by expressing it as a multiple of a measure of variability.
description_plain: Your child can look at two sets of data and estimate how much their values overlap and how different their centers are compared to their spread.

subject: Math
description: Draw informal comparative inferences about two populations.
description_plain: Your child can make informal guesses about differences between two groups based on data.

subject: Math
description: Use data from a random sample to draw inferences about a population with an unknown characteristic of interest. Generate multiple samples (or simulated samples) of the same size to gauge the variation in estimates or predictions.
description_plain: Your child can use data from random samples to make guesses about a larger group and create several samples to see how much the guesses might change.

subject: Math
description: Understand that statistics can be used to gain information about a population by examining a sample of the population; generalizations about a population from a sample are valid only if the sample is representative of that population. Understand that random sampling tends to produce representative samples and support valid inferences.
description_plain: Your child understands that looking at a smaller group (sample) can tell us about a bigger group (population) only if the sample fairly represents the whole group.

subject: Math
description: Use random sampling to draw inferences about a population.
description_plain: Your child can use random samples to make guesses about a larger group.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving area, volume and surface area of two- and three-dimensional objects composed of triangles, quadrilaterals, polygons, cubes, and right prisms.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about area, volume, and surface area of 2D and 3D shapes like triangles, polygons, cubes, and prisms.

subject: Math
description: Use facts about supplementary, complementary, vertical, and adjacent angles in a multi-step problem to write and solve simple equations for an unknown angle in a figure.
description_plain: Your child can use facts about different types of angles to write and solve equations to find unknown angles in shapes.

subject: Math
description: Know the formulas for the area and circumference of a circle and use them to solve problems; give an informal derivation of the relationship between the circumference and area of a circle.
description_plain: Your child knows the formulas for the area and circumference of a circle and can use them to solve problems and understand how they relate.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-life and mathematical problems involving angle measure, area, surface area, and volume.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems involving angles, areas, surface areas, and volumes.

subject: Math
description: Describe the two-dimensional figures resulting from slicing three-dimensional figures, as in plane sections of right rectangular prisms and right rectangular pyramids.
description_plain: Your child can describe the flat shapes made when slicing 3D shapes like boxes and pyramids.

subject: Math
description: Draw (freehand, with ruler and protractor, and with technology) geometric shapes with given conditions. Focus on constructing (drawing?) triangles from three measures of angles or sides, noticing when the conditions determine a unique triangle, more than one triangle, or no triangle.
description_plain: Your child can draw different shapes by hand, with tools, or on a computer, especially triangles using given angles or sides, and understand when these measurements make one, more than one, or no triangle.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving scale drawings of geometric figures, including computing actual lengths and areas from a scale drawing and reproducing a scale drawing at a different scale.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems using scale drawings, finding actual lengths and areas, and can redraw figures at different scales.

subject: Math
description: Draw, construct, and describe geometrical figures and describe the relationships between them.
description_plain: Your child can draw and build shapes and explain how they relate to each other.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems leading to inequalities of the form px + q > r or px + q < r, where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers; graph the solution set of the inequality and interpret it in the context of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that lead to inequalities, show the answers on a graph, and explain what the answers mean.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems leading to equations of the form px + q = r and p(x + q) = r where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers; solve equations of these forms fluently; compare an algebraic solution to an arithmetic solution, identifying the sequence of the operations used in each approach.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that lead to equations, solve them easily, and compare solving with algebra to solving with arithmetic.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent quantities in a real-world or mathematical problem, and construct simple equations and inequalities to solve problems by reasoning about the quantities.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to stand for numbers in real or math problems and write simple equations or inequalities to solve them.

subject: Math
description: Solve multi-step real-life and mathematical problems posed with positive and negative rational numbers in any form (whole numbers, fractions, and decimals), using tools strategically; apply properties of operations to calculate with numbers in any form; convert between forms as appropriate; and assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies.
description_plain: Your child can solve multi-step problems with positive and negative numbers in different forms, using tools and checking if answers make sense.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-life mathematical problems using numerical and algebraic expressions and equations.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life math problems using numbers and simple equations.

subject: Math
description: Understand that rewriting an expression in different forms in a problem context can shed light on the problem and how the quantities in it are related.
description_plain: Your child understands that changing how an expression looks can help understand the problem and how the numbers relate.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add, subtract, factor, and expand linear expressions with rational coefficients.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to add, subtract, factor, and expand expressions with rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use properties of operations to generate equivalent expressions.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to rewrite expressions in different but equal ways.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving the four operations with rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can solve real and math problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Convert a rational number to a decimal using long division; and know that the decimal form of a rational number terminates in 0s or eventually repeats.
description_plain: Your child can change a fraction or rational number into a decimal by dividing and knows decimals either end or repeat.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to multiply and divide rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to multiply and divide rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand that integers can be divided, provided that the divisor is not zero, and every quotient of integers (with non-zero divisor) is a rational number. If p and q are integers, then -(p/q) = -p/q = p/-q. Interpret quotients of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands that dividing whole numbers (except by zero) gives rational numbers and can explain real-life examples of dividing rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand that multiplication is extended from fractions to rational numbers by requiring that operations continue to satisfy the properties of operations, particularly the distributive property, leading to products such as (-1)(-1)= 1 and the rules for multiplying signed numbers. Interpret products of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands how multiplication works with rational numbers, including negatives, and can explain real-world examples.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division and of fractions to multiply and divide rational number.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide rational numbers by building on what they know about fractions.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add and subtract rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to add and subtract rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand subtraction of rational numbers as adding the additive inverse, p - q = p + (-q). Show that the distance between two rational numbers on the number line is the absolute value of their difference, and apply this principle in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands subtracting numbers as adding the opposite and can find the distance between numbers on a number line, using this in real life.

subject: Math
description: Understand p +q as the number located a distance |q| from p, in the positive or negative direction depending on whether q is positive or negative. Show that a number and its opposite have a sum of 0 (are additive inverses). Interpret sums of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands adding positive and negative numbers as moving left or right on a number line and knows that a number plus its opposite equals zero.

subject: Math
description: Describe situations in which opposite quantities combine to make 0.
description_plain: Your child can describe situations where opposite amounts cancel each other out to make zero.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of addition and subtraction to add and subtract rational numbers, and represent addition and subtraction on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract rational numbers and show these operations on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of operations with fractions to add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers, building on what they know about fractions.

subject: Math
description: Use proportional relationships to solve multistep ratio and percent problems.
description_plain: Your child can use proportional relationships to solve multi-step problems involving ratios and percents.

subject: Math
description: Explain what a point (x, y) on the graph of a proportional relationship means in terms of the situation, with special attention to the points (0, 0) and (1, r) where r is the unit rate.
description_plain: Your child can explain what points on a graph mean in a proportional relationship, especially the points (0,0) and (1, unit rate).

subject: Math
description: Represent proportional relationships by equations.
description_plain: Your child can write equations that show proportional relationships.

subject: Math
description: Identify the constant of proportionality (unit rate) in tables, graphs, equations, diagrams and verbal descriptions of proportional relationships.
description_plain: Your child can find the constant unit rate in tables, graphs, equations, pictures, and descriptions of proportional relationships.

subject: Math
description: Decide whether two quantities are in a proportional relationship, e.g., by testing for equivalent ratios in a table or graphing on a coordinate plane and observing whether the graph is a straight line through the origin.
description_plain: Your child can decide if two quantities have a proportional relationship by checking if their ratios are equal or if their graph is a straight line through the origin.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and represent proportional relationships between quantities.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and show proportional relationships between quantities.

subject: Math
description: Compute unit rates associated with ratios of fractions, including ratios of lengths, areas and other quantities measured in like and or different units.
description_plain: Your child can find unit rates for ratios involving fractions, like speed or area, and write them as complex fractions or simpler numbers.

subject: Math
description: Analyze proportional relationships and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can study proportional relationships and use them to solve real-world and math problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describing the impact of geographic expansion on rights for African Americans, Chinese immigrants, and the political and legal rights to women.
description_plain: Your child can describe how moving west affected the rights of African Americans, Chinese immigrants, and women.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluating the impact of government actions on patterns of immigration.
description_plain: Your child can explain how government actions influenced immigration patterns.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluating the American Indian responses to western migration.
description_plain: Your child can explain how American Indians responded to settlers moving west.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describing the impact of geography and technology on the settlement of the west.
description_plain: Your child can explain how geography and new technology affected where and how people settled in the western United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and consequences of westward expansion by:
description_plain: Your child will study the reasons people moved west and what happened because of that expansion.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identifying responses to post-Civil War immigration.
description_plain: Your child can recognize how people reacted to new immigrants after the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluating the impact of industrialization and laissez-faire policies on labor.
description_plain: Your child understands how industrial growth and government policies affected workers and their jobs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describing the impact of business leaders, laissez-faire capitalism, and the use of trusts/monopolies on the American economy.
description_plain: Your child can describe how business leaders, free-market ideas, and big companies influenced the American economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluating how improved use of resources, new technology and inventions, and transportation networks influenced the growth of industrialization and urbanization.
description_plain: Your child understands how better use of resources, inventions, and transportation helped cities and industries grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and consequences of Industrialization in the late 19th century United States by:
description_plain: Your child will explore the causes and effects of industrial growth in the late 1800s in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluating the impact of the Supreme Court, debt peonage, Jim Crow Laws and disenfranchisement on the enforceability of the 13th, 14th, and 15th Amendments.
description_plain: Your child can explain how laws, court decisions, and unfair practices affected the rights given by the 13th, 14th, and 15th Amendments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the major traditions, customs, and beliefs of Islam
description_plain: Your child can study the main traditions, customs, and beliefs of Islam.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the emergence and expansion of Islamic civilization
description_plain: Your child can explain how Islamic civilization began and grew.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the major traditions, customs and beliefs of Hinduism and its political and social impact on India
description_plain: Your child can explain the main traditions, customs, and beliefs of Hinduism and how it affected politics and society in India.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the major traditions, customs and beliefs of Buddhism
description_plain: Your child can describe the main traditions, customs, and beliefs of Buddhism.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the major traditions, customs, and beliefs of Hinduism and Buddhism and their expansion throughout Asia
description_plain: Your child can explain the main traditions, customs, and beliefs of Hinduism and Buddhism and how they spread across Asia.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes of the rise and fall, expansion and contraction of political entities and nation-states
description_plain: Your child can explain why political groups and countries grew or shrank and why they rose or fell.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how interactions promoted or failed to promote development, such as the Fertile Crescent Empire, Greek city-states, and Latin Empires
description_plain: Your child can describe how interactions between groups like the Fertile Crescent Empire, Greek city-states, and Latin Empires helped or didn’t help their growth.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the effect of interactions between civilizations in early world history
description_plain: Your child can explain how early civilizations affected each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the central arguments in secondary works of history on related topics in multiple media
description_plain: Your child can compare main ideas in history books or media about similar topics.

subject: Social Studies
description: Organize applicable evidence into a coherent argument about the past
description_plain: Your child can organize evidence to make a clear argument about history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the relative influence of various causes of events and developments in the past
description_plain: Your child can decide which causes had more or less influence on past events.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain multiple causes and effects of events and developments in the past
description_plain: Your child can explain several causes and effects of events in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the relevancy and utility of a historical source based on information such as maker, date, place of origin, intended audience and purpose
description_plain: Your child can decide if a historical source is useful by looking at who made it, when and where, who it was for, and why it was made.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use questions generated about multiple historical sources to identify further areas of inquiry and additional sources
description_plain: Your child can use questions about different historical sources to find new things to learn and more sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use other historical sources to infer a plausible maker, date, place of origin, and intended audience for historical sources where this information is not easily identified
description_plain: Your child can use other historical sources to guess who made a source, when and where it was made, and who it was for if that information isn’t clear.

subject: Social Studies
description: Detect possible limitations in the historical record based on evidence collected from different kinds of historical sources
description_plain: Your child can find possible gaps or limits in history by looking at different types of historical sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Classify the kinds of historical sources used in a secondary interpretation
description_plain: Your child can sort different kinds of historical sources used when someone explains history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how people's perspectives influenced what information is available in the historical sources they created
description_plain: Your child understands how people’s views affected what information they included in historical records.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how and why perspectives of people have changed over time
description_plain: Your child can explain how and why people’s views have changed over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze multiple factors that influenced the perspectives of people during different historical eras
description_plain: Your child can look at many reasons why people thought differently during various times in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use questions generated about individuals and groups to analyze why they, and the developments they shaped, are seen as historically significant
description_plain: Your child uses questions about people and groups to understand why they and their actions are important in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Classify series of historical events and developments as examples of change and/or continuity
description_plain: Your child can sort historical events into those that show change and those that show things stayed the same.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze connections among events and developments in broader historical contexts
description_plain: Your child can explain how events and developments are connected in larger historical stories.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize the factors that led to the decline of the African empires
description_plain: Your child can summarize the reasons why African empires became weaker and declined.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the cultural and economic impact of African regional and worldwide trade routes
description_plain: Your child can explain how African trade routes affected culture and the economy both regionally and worldwide.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the contributions of major African monarchies, cities, and trade networks, such as Ghana, Mali and Songhai
description_plain: Your child can describe important contributions from African kingdoms, cities, and trade networks like Ghana, Mali, and Songhai.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the emergence, growth and decline of African Empires
description_plain: Your child can study how African empires started, grew, and eventually declined.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze China's cultural, political and economic influence on Japan, Korea, and countries in Southeast Asia
description_plain: Your child can explain how China influenced the culture, politics, and economy of Japan, Korea, and Southeast Asia.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the major traditions, customs and beliefs of Confucianism and Taoism in the context of early Chinese imperial dynasties
description_plain: Your child can explain the main traditions and beliefs of Confucianism and Taoism during early Chinese dynasties.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the causes and consequences of the unification of China under early imperial dynasties, such as the Shang, Zhou, Qin, Han and Tang
description_plain: Your child can describe what caused China to become united under early dynasties like Shang, Zhou, Qin, Han, and Tang, and what happened because of it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the dynasties and empires in ancient China
description_plain: Your child can compare different dynasties and empires in ancient China.

subject: Social Studies
description: Summarize the factors that led to the decline of the Roman Empire
description_plain: Your child can summarize the reasons why the Roman Empire declined.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the transition from Roman Republic to Roman Empire including the social structure, significance of citizenship and the development of political institutions
description_plain: Your child can explain how Rome changed from a republic to an empire, including its social classes, citizenship, and government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the major achievements of the Roman Era, such as legal, artistic, architectural, technological, and literary
description_plain: Your child can describe important achievements from the Roman Era like laws, art, buildings, technology, and literature.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the emergence, expansion and decline of the Roman Empire
description_plain: Your child can study how the Roman Empire started, grew, and eventually fell.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the emergence, rise and decline of the Greek city-states
description_plain: Your child can explain how Greek city-states began, grew, and declined.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the major cultural achievements of the Greek civilization, such as art, science, political systems, and philosophy across time
description_plain: Your child can describe major cultural achievements of ancient Greece like art, science, government, and philosophy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the emergence and enduring influence of Aegean civilizations
description_plain: Your child can study how Aegean civilizations started and why they still influence us today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare major cultural, political and economic achievements of river valley civilizations, such as the Tigris and Euphrates River Valley, the Huang River Valley, the Indus River Valley and the Nile River Valley including Egypt, Nubia and Kush
description_plain: Your child can compare important cultural, political, and economic achievements of river valley civilizations like those by the Tigris and Euphrates, Huang, Indus, and Nile Rivers.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the characteristics of a civilization, such as social hierarchy, government, writing system, specialization in an area of trade and the establishment of cities
description_plain: Your child can describe what makes a civilization, like social classes, government, writing, trade skills, and cities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how civilizations emerged in the river valley areas
description_plain: Your child can study how civilizations began in river valley areas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and trace the development of political and social structures of the Incas, Mayans and Aztecs
description_plain: Your child can describe how the Incas, Mayans, and Aztecs built their political and social systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the emergence, growth and decline of empires in the Americas
description_plain: Your child can study how empires in the Americas started, grew, and declined.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how and why towns and cities grew from early human settlements, including the need for security and government
description_plain: Your child can explain how and why towns and cities grew from early settlements, including the need for safety and government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe characteristics and innovations of hunting and gathering societies, such as nomadic lifestyles, inventors of tools, adaptation to animal migration and vegetation cycles and the shift from food gathering to food-producing activities
description_plain: Your child can describe early hunting and gathering societies, including their nomadic lifestyle, tool-making, and how they shifted to farming.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how the rise of the earliest communities led to the emergence of agricultural societies
description_plain: Your child can study how the first communities led to farming societies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate how social and cultural decisions affect the ecology and economy
description_plain: Your child can explore how social and cultural choices affect the environment and economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use appropriate data to evaluate economic indicators such as, unemployment, inflation, total production, income and economic growth in the economy
description_plain: Your child can use data to understand economic measures like unemployment, inflation, production, income, and growth.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare at least three markets that sell similar goods and services and determine which offers the best value
description_plain: Your child can compare at least three markets selling similar goods or services to decide which gives the best value.

subject: Social Studies
description: Plan and predict the financial outcomes in an applied decision-making process
description_plain: Your child can plan and predict financial results when making decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate alternative approaches or solutions to economic issues in terms of benefits and costs for different groups over time
description_plain: Your child can look at different solutions to economic problems and weigh their benefits and costs for different groups over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how societies used mediums of exchange to facilitate trade and help their economies grow
description_plain: Your child can explain how societies used money or trade items to make trading easier and help their economies grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the importance of medium of exchange in early world history
description_plain: Your child can describe why having a medium of exchange, like money, was important in early world history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how the guild system addressed changes in the economy, such as dictating price and production
description_plain: Your child can explain how guilds helped control prices and production as the economy changed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the growth and management of towns, guilds, and the banking system were affected by technology and agriculture
description_plain: Your child can explain how technology and farming affected the growth and management of towns, guilds, and banks.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the transition from feudalism and the manor system to the development of early towns
description_plain: Your child can explain how feudalism and the manor system changed into early towns.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe examples of command in economies, such as the feudal system
description_plain: Your child can give examples of command economies, like the feudal system.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe examples of tradition in economies such as the economic roles of men and women in tribal societies
description_plain: Your child can describe how traditions shaped roles of men and women in tribal economies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how various early world societies answer the basic question of what, how, and for whom to produce
description_plain: Your child understands how early societies decided what to make, how to make it, and who would get it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the types of economic systems in early world societies
description_plain: Your child can describe different types of economic systems in early world societies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify African and Eurasian trade routes to explain how surplus goods and regional specialization resulted in economic interdependence
description_plain: Your child can identify trade routes in Africa and Eurasia and explain how trading extra goods made regions depend on each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the effects of agricultural surplus and job specialization on the emergence of early towns and cities in various parts of the world
description_plain: Your child can describe how having extra food and special jobs helped early towns and cities grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze examples of regional specialization that resulted from economic resources, such as silk production, bronze casting and terraced farming
description_plain: Your child can explain how certain regions focused on special products like silk, bronze, or farming on hills.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine how specialization, interdependence and trade affected the production of goods and services in early world history
description_plain: Your child understands how people specialized, traded, and depended on each other to make goods and services in early history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine why technology has changed production such as the development of the printing press
description_plain: Your child can explain how new technology, like the printing press, changed how things were made.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of how technology changed consumption and provided greater access to goods and services
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how technology helped people get more goods and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how technological changes affected consumption and production in early world history
description_plain: Your child understands how technology changed what people made and bought in early history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how available resources affected specialization and trade
description_plain: Your child can describe how available resources affected what people specialized in and traded.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify ways people have used resources to meet economic wants such as domesticating agriculture
description_plain: Your child can identify ways people used resources, like farming, to meet their needs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how scarce economic resources were used to satisfy economic wants in early world history
description_plain: Your child understands how people used limited resources to meet their wants in early history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify opportunity cost of economic decisions made by individuals and groups such as the decision to engage in trade
description_plain: Your child can identify what people gave up when making economic choices, like deciding to trade.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain that people made choices because resources relative to economic wants to goods and services in the context of early world history
description_plain: Your child understands that people make choices because resources are limited compared to what they want.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use paper based or electronic mapping and graphing techniques to represent and analyze spatial patterns of different environmental and cultural characteristics
description_plain: Your child can use maps and graphs, on paper or computer, to show and study patterns in the environment and culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use maps, satellite images, photographs, and other representations to explain relationships bet ween the locations of places and regions, and changes in their environmental characteristics
description_plain: Your child can use maps, satellite pictures, and photos to explain how places and their environments are connected and change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct maps to represent and explain the spatial patterns of cultural and environmental characteristics
description_plain: Your child can make maps to show and explain cultural and environmental features in different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how people in early world history perceived and reacted to environmental concerns, such as flooding, drought, and depletion of natural resources and evaluate the consequences of those actions
description_plain: Your child can explain how people long ago noticed and reacted to problems like floods, droughts, and using up resources, and what happened because of their actions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe ways people modified their environment to meet their needs, such as cultivating lands, building roads, dams, and aqueducts
description_plain: Your child can describe how people changed their environment to meet needs, like farming land and building roads or dams.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze why and how people modify their natural environment and the impact of those modifications
description_plain: Your child understands why and how people changed their natural surroundings and what effects those changes had.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how economic systems and cultural diffusion help to shape patterns of human settlement and interaction
description_plain: Your child can describe how economies and sharing cultures affected where people lived and how they interacted.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the development of transportation and communication networks influenced the movement of people, goods and ideas from place to place, such as trade routes in Africa, Asia and Europe, and the spread of Islam
description_plain: Your child can explain how transportation and communication helped people, goods, and ideas move, like trade routes and the spread of Islam.

subject: Social Studies
